---
title: "Suspended Circles"
---

Suspension is the first move from a circular layer toward a sphere-like layer. In this finite model, a circle is given two special pole-like endpoints, and the old circle becomes the equator-like middle layer.

The goal is not to pretend this page has completed smooth sphere geometry. The goal is to show how a finite S1 address system can become part of a higher-dimensional scaffold while keeping the combinatorial facts checkable.

::: {.warning-box}
The widget here is still a placeholder. Treat it as a diagram slot for the finite suspended-circle idea, not as a proof or a complete S2 visualization.
:::

## What To Notice

- The equator-like layer comes from the original finite circle.
- The two added endpoints behave like north/south poles in the finite scaffold.
- Cell counts and endpoint/equator distinctions are the checked claims; continuous metric geometry is future work.

::: {.checkpoint-box}

## Checkpoint

In a finite suspended-circle model, which part comes from the original `C_n`, and which parts are newly added?

:::

## Source Trail

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="S2-T0002"></div>
<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="sphere_grid_placeholder"></div>

<p data-paper-ref="papers/S2/PAPER_S2_01_SUSPENDED_CIRCLES.md">Paper source: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/S2/PAPER_S2_01_SUSPENDED_CIRCLES.md">S2 Suspended Circles</a></p>

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
<script type="module" src="../../widgets/S2/sphere_grid_placeholder.js"></script>
